task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a single string parameter annotated with type hints (`template_str: str`) and return `list[str]`
- The function must raise `TypeError` if the input is not a `str`, using `isinstance(template_str, str)` as the sole check — a separate `None` guard is redundant and must not be used; the error message must explicitly state both the expected type (`str`) and the actual type received (e.g., using `type(template_str).__name__`)
- The function must catch `json.JSONDecodeError` when parsing malformed JSON and raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message that includes context about the parse failure
- The function must validate that the parsed JSON root is a `dict`; if it is any other type (e.g., list, string, number), raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the parsed dict contains a top-level `Resources` key; if the key is absent, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the value of `Resources` is a `dict`; if it is any other type, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message
- The function must validate that the `Resources` dict is non-empty; if it is an empty dict `{}`, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message — returning an empty list for an empty `Resources` dict is explicitly incorrect
- The function must validate that all keys in the `Resources` dict are strings; if any key is a non-string type, raise a `ValueError` with a descriptive message identifying the offending key
- The function must return a `list[str]` of all keys from the `Resources` dictionary as the logical IDs when all validations pass
- The function must include a docstring describing the expected input format, return value, and every exception (`TypeError`, `ValueError`) that may be raised, including the specific condition that triggers each

task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules
solution_criteria:
The function must accept a string and return True only if the name satisfies ALL of the following AWS S3 bucket naming rules, and False otherwise:
- If the input is not a string (e.g., None, int, list), immediately return False without raising any exception — use an isinstance(name, str) guard as the very first statement in the function body
- Length must be between 3 and 63 characters (inclusive); enforce this exclusively within the regex pattern (do not add a separate len() guard — the regex alone is the authoritative length check); verify edge cases: 'ab' (2 chars) must return False, 'abc' (3 chars) must return True, a 63-character valid name must return True, and a 64-character valid name must return False
- The main validation regex must be compiled once at module level as a named constant (e.g., BUCKET_PATTERN = re.compile(...)) and reused on each call — do not call re.compile() or re.match() with a raw string literal inside the function body
- Only lowercase letters (a-z), digits (0-9), and hyphens (-) are allowed; dots (.) are not permitted in this implementation
- Must start and end with a lowercase letter or digit (not a hyphen)
- Must not contain consecutive hyphens (e.g., 'my--bucket' is invalid); enforce this rule directly inside the main regex using a negative lookahead (e.g., (?!.*--)) rather than a separate 'if "--" in name' check outside the regex
- The main regex must therefore combine in a single pattern: the length bounds (3-63), the allowed character set, the start/end character constraints, and the consecutive-hyphen prohibition via negative lookahead
- A dedicated IP_PATTERN compiled regex constant must be present at module level as a safeguard; however, because dots are already excluded by BUCKET_PATTERN, explicitly document in both the docstring and an inline comment that any name passing BUCKET_PATTERN can never match IP_PATTERN — the IP check must still be called in the function body but its logical redundancy must be acknowledged, not silently hidden
- Must include a docstring that lists each validation rule being enforced, explicitly states that the IPv4 check is inherently satisfied by the no-dots rule, and notes that non-string inputs return False
- Must not use redundant or conflicting length checks (e.g., do not combine a regex length quantifier with a separate len() call)

task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs
solution_criteria:
- The regex must match all three AWS partitions: `arn:aws`, `arn:aws-us-gov`, and `arn:aws-cn`, using a named capture group called `partition` that matches only these three exact values
- The account ID must be captured in a named group called `account_id` and match exactly 12 decimal digits — no more, no fewer
- The role name must be captured in a named group called `role_name`; the character class for valid role name characters **must be explicitly written as `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]`** (with the forward slash `/` included inside the character class itself, not used only as a separator outside the class)
- Each path segment in the role name (when split by `/`) must be non-empty, meaning consecutive forward slashes (e.g., `role//name`) must not match; enforce this by requiring each segment to contain at least one non-slash character — the recommended pattern is `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]+` for the full role name combined with a negative lookahead `(?!.*//)`  or equivalent segment structure such as `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+(/[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+)*` where the base class still reflects the full allowed set
- The forward slash `/` must appear inside the character class `[a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]` as a literal member of the class — solutions that use `/` only as a structural separator between alternation groups (e.g., `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+(/[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+)*`) without including `/` in the class itself do not satisfy this requirement
- The `match_arn` function must accept a single argument and return `None` (without raising any exception) when the input is `None`, an empty string, a non-string type, or any string that does not match the ARN pattern; on a successful match it must return the `re.Match` object
- All unit tests must be written using `pytest` or `unittest` and must not execute at import time; no test code or test function calls may appear at module level outside of a `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard or a dedicated test file
- Include at least five unit tests covering: a standard `arn:aws` ARN (assert correct `partition`, `account_id`, and `role_name` capture group values), a GovCloud `arn:aws-us-gov` ARN, a China `arn:aws-cn` ARN, an invalid ARN with wrong format (assert no match), and a `None` or empty string input (assert return value is `None`)
- Include one additional test asserting that a role name containing consecutive slashes (e.g., `role//name`) does not match the pattern
- Include one additional test asserting that a role name containing a forward slash in a valid path (e.g., `path/role-name`) does match the pattern and that the full `role_name` capture group value includes the slash

task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys
solution_criteria:
- Accept input as either a pre-parsed dict or a raw JSON string, using json.loads() to parse string input before processing
- Return a flat dictionary where all nested keys are joined with dot-notation (e.g., 'network.interfaces.0.mac') and leaf values are preserved as-is
- Raise a TypeError with a descriptive message if input is None or not a dict or string type; this validation must occur unconditionally at every recursive call, not only at depth 0, so that any non-dict value reaching data.items() produces a clean TypeError rather than an AttributeError
- Raise a ValueError with a descriptive message if a string input is not valid JSON
- After parsing a JSON string, if the parsed result is not a dict, raise a TypeError with a message that explicitly distinguishes this case from a non-string/non-dict input — the message must indicate that the JSON string was valid but did not decode to a dict (e.g., 'JSON string decoded to <type> instead of dict')
- Include a recursion depth guard that raises a RecursionError with a descriptive message when _depth reaches exactly 20 (i.e., use _depth >= 20 so that depth 20 is the first disallowed level, making 19 the true maximum allowed depth); the depth check must occur before any string parsing so that a string input at depth 20 raises RecursionError rather than ValueError, and this ordering must be explicitly documented in the docstring
- List values encountered during flattening must not be recursively expanded with numeric indices; instead they must be stored as-is as leaf values, and this design decision must be explicitly documented in the docstring with a note explaining why (e.g., EC2 metadata arrays are treated as atomic values)
- Include a complete docstring describing all parameters including the internal _depth parameter (its type, default value, and purpose), the return type, all raised exceptions including the post-parse TypeError for non-dict JSON, the depth-check-before-parse ordering, the list-as-leaf behavior, and an example using EC2 metadata structure
- Include type hints for all parameters and the return value, including _depth: int = 0 in the signature (e.g., def flatten_metadata(data: Union[dict, str], prefix: str = '', _depth: int = 0) -> dict)

task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files
solution_criteria:
- The regex must use a single capture group (parentheses) wrapping the entire 20-character key so the full key is returned via match group(1) or equivalent; no additional capture groups may split the key
- The pattern must match all four common AWS key ID prefixes — AKIA, ASIA, AROA, and AIDA — using a non-capturing alternation group (e.g., (?:AKIA|ASIA|AROA|AIDA))
- After the 4-character prefix, the pattern must match exactly 16 uppercase alphanumeric characters ([A-Z0-9]{16}), for a total captured length of exactly 20 characters
- The pattern must use a negative lookbehind (?<![A-Z0-9]) immediately before the prefix and a negative lookahead (?![A-Z0-9]) immediately after the 16-character suffix to prevent matching keys embedded within longer alphanumeric strings (e.g., 'XAKIA...' or 'AKIA...EXTRA' must not match); reliance on \b alone is insufficient and must not be used as the sole boundary assertion
- The pattern must be case-sensitive and match only uppercase characters; the solution must explicitly compile or apply the regex without re.IGNORECASE or any equivalent flag, and a code comment must confirm this omission is intentional
- The solution must include a test case with a fully lowercase key variant (e.g., 'akiaiosfodnn7example') and explicitly assert that no match is returned, demonstrating case-sensitivity rejection
- All test cases must use explicit assert statements (or equivalent programmatic pass/fail logic) rather than relying solely on print output, so that failures raise an error or exception automatically
- The solution must demonstrate the pattern correctly rejects strings where the key is preceded or followed by additional word characters (e.g., 'XAKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE' and 'AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLEXYZ' must both produce no match via explicit assertions)

task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format
solution_criteria:
- Accept a single parameter typed as `int` with a descriptive name (e.g., `timestamp_ms`), and include a complete docstring describing the parameter, return value, any exceptions raised, and explicitly noting that `0` (Unix epoch) is a valid input
- Validate the input type using a check that explicitly excludes booleans: use `if not isinstance(timestamp_ms, int) or isinstance(timestamp_ms, bool)` to raise `TypeError`; the error message must mention both the expected type and that booleans are excluded (e.g., `"timestamp_ms must be an int (bool not accepted)"`)
- Handle `None` explicitly before the general type check, raising `TypeError` with a dedicated descriptive message such as `"timestamp_ms must not be None"`
- Raise `ValueError` if the value is negative or exceeds a defined upper bound representing year 9999 in milliseconds (e.g., `253402300799999`); define this upper bound as a named constant inside the function body (e.g., `MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS = 253402300799999`) to keep it encapsulated and self-documenting; the error message must state the valid range including that `0` is the minimum accepted value
- Convert the milliseconds timestamp to seconds before constructing a UTC-aware `datetime` object using `datetime.fromtimestamp(ts_seconds, tz=timezone.utc)`
- Format the output using `strftime` with an explicit format string (e.g., `"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`) to produce a consistent ISO 8601 string without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
- Include type hints for both the parameter (`int`) and the return value (`str`)
- Explicitly verify in the docstring or inline comments that `timestamp_ms == 0` returns `"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"`, confirming the Unix epoch edge case is handled correctly

task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object
solution_criteria:
The JSON schema should define required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) with correct data types, and must satisfy the following precise requirements:
- FunctionName must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9-_:]+$ and have maxLength of 140 to support both plain names (up to 64 chars) and full ARN format (up to 140 chars)
- Runtime must be an enum restricted to valid AWS Lambda runtime strings (e.g., python3.11, nodejs18.x, java17, etc.)
- Role must match an ARN pattern such as ^arn:aws:iam::[0-9]{12}:role/.+$
- Handler must match the pattern ^[a-zA-Z0-9_/.-]+\.[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$ to enforce the AWS 'filename.method' format and have a maxLength of 128
- VpcConfig, when present, must require SubnetIds and SecurityGroupIds as arrays each with minItems of 1, and must set additionalProperties to false so that no unknown fields within VpcConfig pass validation silently
- EphemeralStorage must be supported as an optional field with a nested Size property (integer, minimum 512, maximum 10240), and the EphemeralStorage object must set additionalProperties to false to prevent extra properties from passing validation silently
- Environment must contain a Variables object where: (1) all keys must match pattern ^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ via patternProperties, (2) all values must be explicitly typed as strings in the patternProperties value schema, (3) additionalProperties must be set to false on the Variables object to reject non-matching keys, and (4) maxProperties of 128 must be enforced on the Variables object; additionally, the Environment object itself must set additionalProperties to false to prevent any sibling keys alongside Variables from passing validation silently
- Timeout must be an integer with minimum 1 and maximum 900, and MemorySize must be an integer with minimum 128 and maximum 10240
- The top-level schema must set additionalProperties to false to prevent unknown or invalid Lambda configuration fields from passing validation silently

task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition
solution_criteria:
- The function must accept a list of DynamoDB item dictionaries in boto3 typed format (e.g., `{'attr': {'S': 'value'}}` or `{'attr': {'N': '123'}}`) and a condition specified as an attribute name and a plain Python value, returning only items where the typed attribute value matches the condition value after extracting the inner typed value
- The function must support at minimum the DynamoDB types `S` (string), `N` (numeric string compared using `decimal.Decimal` for both the stored value and the comparison value to avoid float precision issues and remain consistent with boto3/DynamoDB conventions), and `BOOL` (boolean)
- The function must explicitly document in the docstring that only the first key of a typed value dict is evaluated (consistent with DynamoDB's single-type-per-attribute format), and the implementation must reflect this intentional design rather than relying on implicit loop behavior
- The function must validate inputs and raise a `ValueError` if the items list is `None`; non-dict entries in the items list must be silently skipped and must never raise a `TypeError` — the docstring must accurately reflect this behavior by stating non-dict entries are silently skipped with no exception raised
- The isinstance check for dict items must appear exactly once in the implementation — either in the filtering logic or in the match helper, not in both
- When an `N`-type comparison is attempted and the `condition_value` cannot be converted to `decimal.Decimal` (e.g., it is a non-numeric string), the function must catch `decimal.InvalidOperation` and treat the item as non-matching (exclude it from results), and this behavior must be documented in the docstring
- The function must include full type hints on all parameters and the return value
- The function must include a docstring describing parameters, return value, supported DynamoDB types (`S`, `N`, `BOOL`), explicitly unsupported types (`L`, `M`, `SS`, `NS`, `BS`, `NULL`), the single-key evaluation behavior, the silent skipping of non-dict list entries, the exclusion behavior for unsupported DynamoDB type keys, the exclusion behavior when `condition_value` cannot be converted to `Decimal` for `N`-type comparisons, and any exceptions raised
- Unsupported DynamoDB type keys encountered during filtering must cause the item to be excluded from results (not raise an exception), and this behavior must be documented in the docstring

task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints
solution_criteria:
The regex must match standard AWS region codes (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1) as well as AWS GovCloud regions in three-segment format (e.g., us-gov-west-1, us-gov-east-1).
- The region pattern must explicitly model two distinct alternates inside the named capture group: a two-segment format ([a-z]+-[a-z]+-\d+) for standard regions and a three-segment format ([a-z]+-gov-[a-z]+-\d+) for GovCloud regions, rather than making '-gov' optional on a single pattern, to prevent partial or ambiguous matches.
- The left boundary must be enforced using a non-consuming lookbehind or lookahead such that the literal dot preceding the region is NOT consumed by the match — specifically, use (?:(?:^|(?<=\.))) or a zero-width assertion so that re.findall() and re.search() both return only the named group content without including surrounding dots or URL components in any capture group.
- The right boundary of the region match must require the region to be immediately followed by the literal string \.amazonaws\.com (with optional additional dot-separated subdomains permitted after that suffix via a non-capturing group), and this suffix must be matched as a required, non-optional component — not guarded by ? — so that region-like strings not followed by .amazonaws.com are never captured.
- The regex must use a named capture group (?P<region>...) that isolates only the region code string, excluding any surrounding dots, URL prefixes, or suffixes.
- The geographic prefix component must use [a-z]+ (one or more lowercase letters) rather than a fixed-length quantifier such as {2,3}, so that prefixes of any length are matched without hardcoding expected lengths.
- The directional or sub-region component (e.g., 'east', 'west', 'southeast', 'northeast') must also use [a-z]+ to accommodate multi-character directional tokens of any length.
- The solution must be implemented as a single compiled Python pattern assigned to a variable via re.compile(...) with a complete, syntactically valid raw string literal — the string must have both an opening and closing quote and the re.compile() call must have a matching closing parenthesis — so that the pattern compiles without SyntaxError or TypeError.
- The pattern must be directly usable with re.search() or re.findall() against a full URL string with no helper functions or multi-step parsing required.
- The pattern must not produce false positives on dot-separated URL segments that resemble region codes but are not followed by .amazonaws.com (e.g., 's3', 'bucket', 'execute-api' must not be captured as regions).

task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
solution_criteria:
- Accept `size_bytes` (int/float) and `storage_class` (str) parameters, plus optional `num_objects` (default 1) and `actual_duration_fraction` (float, default 1.0) parameters; raise `ValueError` for negative `size_bytes`, for `num_objects` less than or equal to zero, or for unrecognized `storage_class` values; accept any numeric type for `num_objects` (int, float, numpy integer, etc.) as long as its value is a positive number — do not restrict by Python type alone
- Support storage classes: `STANDARD`, `STANDARD_IA`, `ONEZONE_IA`, `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, `GLACIER`, `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`
- Apply tiered pricing for `STANDARD` (first 50TB at $0.023/GB, next 450TB at $0.022/GB, over 500TB at $0.021/GB) with no minimum duration and no `actual_duration_fraction` scaling — STANDARD cost is always computed as a full-month flat cost regardless of `actual_duration_fraction`; document this explicitly in the docstring
- All non-STANDARD classes use flat per-GB rates defined as named constants with inline comments noting values may become stale and should be verified at https://aws.amazon.com/s3/pricing/; define `GLACIER_IR_RATE = 0.004` (per GB per month, matching AWS Glacier Instant Retrieval pricing as of 2024)
- Define `DAYS_IN_MONTH = 30.44` and `IA_MIN_DAYS = 30`; for `STANDARD_IA` and `ONEZONE_IA`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB and compute monthly cost as `max(effective_size_gb, IA_MIN_SIZE_GB) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, IA_MIN_DAYS / DAYS_IN_MONTH)` — this bills for at least 30/30.44 ≈ 0.9856 of a month, not a full month
- For `GLACIER_INSTANT_RETRIEVAL`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 128KB and a 90-day minimum storage duration; compute as `max(effective_size_gb, GLACIER_IR_MIN_SIZE_GB) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, 90 / DAYS_IN_MONTH)`
- For `GLACIER`, enforce a minimum billable object size of 48KB (40KB data + 8KB overhead) and a 90-day minimum storage duration; for `GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE`, enforce the same 48KB minimum and a 180-day minimum storage duration; apply `max(effective_size_gb, min_size_gb) * rate * max(actual_duration_fraction, min_days / DAYS_IN_MONTH)` for both
- For `INTELLIGENT_TIERING`, charge storage at the frequent access tier rate ($0.023/GB) applied as a full-month flat cost with no `actual_duration_fraction` scaling (consistent with STANDARD), plus a monitoring and automation fee of `num_objects * ($0.0025 / 1000)` USD per month that is also not scaled by `actual_duration_fraction` since monitoring is billed per object per month regardless of duration; document in the docstring that the frequent access tier rate is assumed, the infrequent access tier rate ($0.0125/GB) is not blended, and callers needing blended rates must supply a weighted `size_bytes`
- Return the total monthly cost in USD as a float rounded to 10 decimal places
